text: A Python UDF needs to call an external REST API (e.g., Google Translate) from within Snowflake. Which object aggregates the allowed network destinations and authentication secrets so the UDF can make outbound HTTPS requests?

options.A: A storage integration with the API endpoint URL
options.B: An external access integration specifying ALLOWED_NETWORK_RULES and ALLOWED_AUTHENTICATION_SECRETS
options.C: A network policy with the API IP range in ALLOWED_IP_LIST
options.D: An API integration with API_PROVIDER = rest_api

explanation: External access integrations (CREATE EXTERNAL ACCESS INTEGRATION) aggregate network rules (egress destinations) and secrets (credentials). UDFs/procedures reference the integration via EXTERNAL_ACCESS_INTEGRATIONS to make outbound HTTPS calls. Network policies control inbound access. Storage integrations are for cloud storage. API integrations with API_PROVIDER are for external functions, not UDF outbound calls.

text: An administrator needs to sample approximately 5% of a 10-billion-row table for a quick exploratory analysis. They want consistent results across re-runs. Which SQL construct achieves deterministic fractional sampling?

options.A: SELECT * FROM t LIMIT 500000000
options.B: SELECT * FROM t SAMPLE SYSTEM (5) SEED (42)
options.C: SELECT * FROM t WHERE MOD(HASH(id), 20) = 0
options.D: SELECT * FROM t ORDER BY RANDOM() LIMIT 500000000

explanation: SAMPLE SYSTEM (5) SEED (42) applies block-level sampling at 5% probability with a fixed seed for deterministic results. LIMIT returns the first N rows (non-random). MOD/HASH is a manual workaround but not the standard SAMPLE construct. ORDER BY RANDOM() LIMIT is expensive on 10B rows.

text: An engineer wants to track changes to a table without creating a persistent stream object. They use SELECT * FROM t CHANGES(INFORMATION => DEFAULT) AT(TIMESTAMP => $ts). What must be true for this to return data?

options.A: A stream must already exist on the table
options.B: CHANGE_TRACKING must be enabled on the table (or a stream must exist which implicitly enables it)
options.C: The table must be a dynamic table with TARGET_LAG configured
options.D: The query must be inside an explicit transaction

explanation: The CHANGES clause requires change tracking metadata. This is enabled either by ALTER TABLE SET CHANGE_TRACKING = TRUE or by creating a stream on the table (which implicitly enables tracking). Unlike streams, CHANGES does not advance any offset. Dynamic tables and explicit transactions are not requirements.

text: A table uses TIME_TRAVEL retention of 30 days. An INSERT happens at Day 1. At Day 15, the engineer queries: SELECT * FROM t AT(OFFSET => -86400). What time point does OFFSET => -86400 reference?

options.A: 86400 rows ago in the table's history
options.B: 86400 seconds (24 hours) before the current time
options.C: 86400 micro-partitions back from the current version
options.D: Day 1, which is 86400 hours before Day 15

explanation: AT(OFFSET => -N) specifies N seconds before the current time. -86400 = 24 hours ago. It is NOT rows, micro-partitions, or hours. This is a common exam trap — the unit is always SECONDS for the OFFSET parameter.

text: A data team uses Snowflake Notebooks for interactive analysis. The notebook contains SQL cells, Python cells, and Markdown cells. Where does the Python code in a notebook cell execute?

options.A: On the user's local browser runtime via WebAssembly
options.B: On the virtual warehouse attached to the notebook session, or on a container runtime
options.C: On a dedicated Snowflake Notebooks compute pool shared across all users
options.D: On the Cloud Services layer alongside query optimization

explanation: Snowflake Notebooks execute Python code on the compute resource attached to the session — either a virtual warehouse (for warehouse-based notebooks) or a Snowpark Container Services runtime. Code does not run in the browser, on a shared pool, or on Cloud Services.

text: An engineer creates: CREATE TABLE t1 (id INT, data VARIANT) CLUSTER BY (id). Later they run ALTER TABLE t1 CLUSTER BY (data:region::VARCHAR, id). What happens to the existing clustering?

options.A: The new clustering key is added alongside the original — both are maintained
options.B: The new clustering key REPLACES the old one entirely. Snowflake begins re-clustering based on the new key.
options.C: The ALTER fails because VARIANT paths cannot be used in clustering keys
options.D: The original clustering is suspended but not removed until explicitly dropped

explanation: ALTER TABLE ... CLUSTER BY replaces the existing clustering key entirely. Only one clustering key definition exists per table at any time. VARIANT paths (cast to scalar types) ARE supported in clustering keys. Automatic clustering then begins reorganizing data based on the new key.

text: Which TWO object types support Time Travel (querying historical data via AT/BEFORE)? (Select TWO)

options.A: Permanent tables
options.B: Named internal stages
options.C: Transient tables (up to 1 day)
options.D: Pipes

explanation: Time Travel works on permanent tables (up to 90 days on Enterprise+) and transient tables (up to 1 day). Stages, pipes, and warehouses do not support Time Travel queries. Temporary tables also support Time Travel (0-1 day) but are not listed as an option.

text: A Snowflake ML Function FORECAST is trained on historical sales data and produces predictions for the next 30 days. Where are the model artifacts stored and what compute trains the model?

options.A: Model stored in the Model Registry; trained on the user's warehouse
options.B: Model stored internally by Snowflake (serverless); trained on serverless compute — no user warehouse needed
options.C: Model stored as a file on a user stage; trained on a Snowpark Container
options.D: Model stored in a dynamic table; no separate training step exists

explanation: ML Functions (FORECAST, ANOMALY_DETECTION) are fully serverless — both training and inference happen on Snowflake-managed compute. Model artifacts are stored internally by Snowflake, not in the Model Registry or on user stages. No warehouse is required.

text: A developer writes a JavaScript stored procedure that calls snowflake.execute() to run CREATE TABLE and DROP TABLE. Can a JavaScript UDF perform the same operations?

options.A: Yes — JavaScript UDFs have the same capabilities as stored procedures
options.B: No — UDFs cannot execute DDL or DML statements; they can only compute and return values
options.C: Yes — but only if the UDF is defined with EXECUTE AS OWNER
options.D: No — but only because JavaScript UDFs are deprecated in favor of Python UDFs

explanation: UDFs cannot execute DDL (CREATE, DROP) or DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE). They are pure computation functions that receive inputs and return values. Only stored procedures can call snowflake.execute() to run SQL statements. JavaScript UDFs are not deprecated.

text: An account has STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS set to 3600 at the account level. A user's session sets it to 7200. A query in that session takes 5000 seconds. Is it canceled?

options.A: Yes — the account-level 3600 takes precedence and cancels it at 3600 seconds
options.B: No — the session-level 7200 overrides the account-level setting (most specific wins), and 5000 < 7200
options.C: Yes — the lower of the two values always applies
options.D: No — STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS cannot be set at session level

explanation: Snowflake's parameter hierarchy: object level > session level > account level. The most specific setting wins. Session set 7200 overrides account 3600. Since 5000 < 7200, the query is not canceled. If the session hadn't overridden it, the query would have been canceled at 3600.

text: A stored procedure is created with EXECUTE AS CALLER. The procedure attempts CREATE TABLE in a schema where only the procedure's OWNER has CREATE TABLE privilege. What happens when a user without CREATE TABLE calls the procedure?

options.A: The CREATE TABLE succeeds because the procedure owner's privileges apply
options.B: The CREATE TABLE fails because EXECUTE AS CALLER uses the caller's privileges
options.C: The procedure compilation fails at creation time
options.D: The CREATE TABLE succeeds but the table is owned by the caller

explanation: EXECUTE AS CALLER means all SQL inside the procedure runs with the calling user's privileges. If the caller lacks CREATE TABLE, the DDL fails. EXECUTE AS OWNER (default) would use the owner's privileges. This distinction is critical for security-sensitive procedures.

text: An analyst queries SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY but receives zero rows even though they ran queries this week. What is the most likely cause?

options.A: QUERY_HISTORY has a 7-day retention limit
options.B: The analyst's role has not been granted IMPORTED PRIVILEGES on the SNOWFLAKE database
options.C: ACCOUNT_USAGE views require Business Critical Edition
options.D: QUERY_HISTORY is available only via INFORMATION_SCHEMA

explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE views in the SNOWFLAKE shared database require IMPORTED PRIVILEGES granted to the querying role. Without this grant, queries return zero rows (not an error). Retention is 365 days. Available in all editions. INFORMATION_SCHEMA is a separate, database-scoped alternative.

text: A masking policy uses CURRENT_ROLE() to determine whether to mask data. A user activates secondary roles via USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL. Their primary role is DATA_ANALYST (masked) but they also have DATA_ENGINEER (unmasked) as a secondary role. What value does the user see?

options.A: Unmasked — secondary roles expand the user's effective privileges
options.B: Masked — CURRENT_ROLE() returns only the primary role, and the policy evaluates against it
options.C: An error because masking policies are incompatible with secondary roles
options.D: Unmasked — IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() is automatically used instead of CURRENT_ROLE()

explanation: CURRENT_ROLE() always returns ONLY the primary role regardless of secondary roles. The masking policy checks CURRENT_ROLE() = 'DATA_ANALYST', which matches, so data is masked. To account for secondary roles, policies should use IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() instead. This is a subtle but important design consideration.

text: A data engineer creates a SECRET object containing an OAuth refresh token for use in a UDF that calls an external API. What type of Snowflake object is a SECRET?

options.A: A session variable that expires when the session ends
options.B: A schema-level object that securely stores credentials for use by external access integrations and UDFs
options.C: An encryption key stored in the account's HSM
options.D: A temporary token generated per-query by the Cloud Services layer

explanation: SECRET is a schema-level, persistent object (CREATE SECRET) that securely stores credentials — OAuth tokens, generic strings, or passwords. Secrets are referenced by external access integrations and UDFs to authenticate with external services. They persist across sessions and are not encryption keys or temporary tokens.

text: An administrator runs GRANT OWNERSHIP ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA s1 TO ROLE new_owner REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS. An existing GRANT SELECT on table t1 to ROLE analyst existed before the transfer. Does the analyst still have SELECT on t1?

options.A: Yes — ownership transfer does not affect existing grants to other roles
options.B: No — REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS removes ALL existing privilege grants during ownership transfer
options.C: Yes — but only for 24 hours until a metadata refresh
options.D: No — but only the previous owner's self-grants are revoked

explanation: REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS removes ALL existing privilege grants on the objects during ownership transfer — including grants to roles other than the previous owner. To preserve grants, use COPY CURRENT GRANTS instead. This is the default behavior if neither is specified.

text: Which TWO views are found in SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE (NOT in INFORMATION_SCHEMA)? (Select TWO)

options.A: LOGIN_HISTORY — authentication event tracking
options.B: TABLES — list of tables in the current database
options.C: WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY — credit consumption by warehouse
options.D: COLUMNS — column metadata for the current database
options.E: SCHEMATA — schemas in the current database

explanation: LOGIN_HISTORY and WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY are ACCOUNT_USAGE-only views (365-day retention, account-wide scope, latency). TABLES, COLUMNS, and SCHEMATA exist in both ACCOUNT_USAGE and INFORMATION_SCHEMA, but the INFORMATION_SCHEMA versions are real-time and database-scoped.

text: A compliance requirement mandates that all data at rest uses a composite encryption key combining Snowflake's key with a customer-managed key. Which Snowflake feature enables this, and what is the minimum edition?

options.A: Column-level encryption, available in Enterprise Edition
options.B: Tri-Secret Secure, available in Business Critical Edition or higher
options.C: AES-256 with customer-provided keys, available in all editions
options.D: PrivateLink encryption, available in Business Critical Edition

explanation: Tri-Secret Secure creates a composite encryption key from Snowflake's key + a customer-managed key (CMK). If either key is unavailable, data cannot be decrypted. This requires Business Critical Edition or higher. AES-256 is standard in all editions but uses only Snowflake-managed keys. PrivateLink is for network security, not encryption.

text: A CSV file contains fields with embedded commas wrapped in double quotes: '"Smith, John"'. COPY INTO incorrectly splits this into two columns. Which file format option correctly handles quoted fields?

options.A: ESCAPE = '\\'
options.B: FIELD_OPTIONALLY_ENCLOSED_BY = '"'
options.C: FIELD_DELIMITER = '|'
options.D: SKIP_HEADER = 1

explanation: FIELD_OPTIONALLY_ENCLOSED_BY = '"' tells Snowflake that field values may be enclosed in double quotes, and any delimiter characters within the quotes are treated as literal data. This correctly parses 'Smith, John' as a single field value.

text: A GCS bucket receives new Parquet files every minute. Snowpipe AUTO_INGEST = TRUE is configured. Which GCP service delivers the file-arrival notification to Snowflake?

options.A: Cloud Functions triggered by GCS object creation
options.B: Google Cloud Pub/Sub subscription
options.C: GCS Transfer Service scheduled pull
options.D: Dataflow streaming pipeline

explanation: Snowpipe AUTO_INGEST on GCP uses Google Cloud Pub/Sub to deliver GCS object event notifications to Snowflake. On AWS it is SQS. On Azure it is Event Grid. Cloud Functions, Transfer Service, and Dataflow are not part of the Snowpipe trigger mechanism.

text: An engineer runs PUT file:///tmp/data.csv @~/uploads/ from SnowSQL. What compression is applied to the file by default before upload?

options.A: ZSTD
options.B: GZIP
options.C: LZO
options.D: No compression — files are uploaded raw by default

explanation: PUT auto-compresses files with GZIP by default (AUTO_COMPRESS = TRUE). The file is compressed client-side before transmission to the stage. To disable, set AUTO_COMPRESS = FALSE. Other formats (ZSTD, LZO) are not the default.

text: A task's body references a stream: INSERT INTO target SELECT * FROM my_stream WHERE 1=1. The task's WHEN clause is: SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA('my_stream'). On a run where the stream has zero new rows, what happens?

options.A: The task runs but inserts zero rows
options.B: The task is skipped entirely — WHEN evaluates to FALSE and zero compute is consumed
options.C: The task errors because the stream is empty
options.D: The task runs and the warehouse is charged for the minimum 60 seconds

explanation: SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA returns FALSE when no new changes exist. The WHEN clause evaluates to FALSE, and the task is skipped entirely — no warehouse is resumed, no compute consumed. This pattern is standard for avoiding unnecessary task executions on empty streams.

text: COPY INTO loads Parquet files using MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME = CASE_INSENSITIVE. The Parquet file has column 'Order_Amount' and the target table has column 'ORDER_AMOUNT'. Does the mapping succeed?

options.A: No — Parquet column names are always case-sensitive
options.B: Yes — CASE_INSENSITIVE matches regardless of case differences between file and table columns
options.C: No — MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME only works with CSV, not Parquet
options.D: Yes — but only if both names are uppercase

explanation: MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME = CASE_INSENSITIVE matches file column names to table column names regardless of case. 'Order_Amount' matches 'ORDER_AMOUNT'. This works with Parquet, Avro, ORC, and CSV (with PARSE_HEADER).

text: During an unload (COPY INTO @stage FROM table), an engineer sets MAX_FILE_SIZE = 200000000. What does this control?

options.A: The maximum total size of all output files combined
options.B: The approximate upper limit in bytes for each individual output file
options.C: The maximum memory allocated per unload thread
options.D: The maximum size of the source data that can be unloaded

explanation: MAX_FILE_SIZE sets the approximate upper limit (in bytes) for each individual file generated during unload. 200000000 ≈ 200 MB per file. When reached, a new file is started. It does not limit total output, memory, or source data size.

text: Files loaded via Snowpipe 15 days ago are re-put to the same stage path with identical names. Will Snowpipe reload them automatically?

options.A: No — Snowpipe permanently remembers all files it has ever loaded
options.B: Yes — Snowpipe metadata expires after 14 days, so these files are now treated as new
options.C: No — Snowpipe uses file checksums to detect duplicates indefinitely
options.D: Yes — but only if the file content has changed

explanation: Snowpipe load metadata expires after 14 days. Files loaded more than 14 days ago are no longer tracked. If the same filename reappears, Snowpipe treats it as a new file and loads it again. This differs from bulk COPY's 64-day metadata retention.

text: Which TWO stage types are automatically created for every Snowflake user and table without an explicit CREATE STAGE command? (Select TWO)

options.A: User stage (@~)
options.B: Table stage (@%table_name)
options.C: Named internal stage
options.D: Named external stage

explanation: Every user automatically gets a user stage (@~) and every table gets a table stage (@%table_name). These exist without any CREATE STAGE. Named internal/external stages must be explicitly created. Directory table stages are not a separate stage type.

text: An engineer writes COPY INTO t FROM (SELECT $1, $2, METADATA$FILENAME FROM @stg) FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE=CSV). They later try SELECT METADATA$FILENAME FROM @stg in a standalone query. What happens?

options.A: Both queries work — METADATA$ columns are available in any stage query
options.B: The standalone query fails — METADATA$ pseudo-columns are only available within COPY INTO transformations
options.C: Both fail — METADATA$ columns require a directory table
options.D: The standalone query returns NULL instead of the filename

explanation: METADATA$FILENAME and METADATA$FILE_ROW_NUMBER are pseudo-columns available ONLY within COPY INTO SELECT transformations. They cannot be used in standalone queries against stages. For file metadata outside COPY, use directory tables (if enabled on the stage).

text: A team runs an ETL job on a Medium warehouse that takes 4 hours. They switch to a Large warehouse. Approximately how long will the same job take, assuming it is scan/compute-bound?

options.A: 4 hours — size does not affect single-query performance
options.B: ~2 hours — doubling compute roughly halves execution time for scan-heavy workloads
options.C: Exactly 1 hour — each size provides 4x improvement
options.D: ~30 minutes — Large is 4x the credits, so 4x faster

explanation: Doubling warehouse size (M→L = 4→8 credits/hr) roughly doubles compute power. For scan-heavy workloads, this approximately halves execution time. The relationship is not perfectly linear due to coordination overhead, but ~2 hours is the expected approximation.

text: A 50 TB table has no clustering key. An analyst frequently queries WHERE email = 'specific@example.com'. The email column has 500 million distinct values. Which optimization is BEST for this access pattern?

options.A: Add a clustering key on email
options.B: Enable Search Optimization Service on the email column
options.C: Create a materialized view with a WHERE clause on email
options.D: Increase warehouse size to 4XL

explanation: SOS is specifically designed for equality/IN lookups on HIGH-cardinality columns (like email with 500M distinct values). Clustering keys are optimal for low-medium cardinality columns with range filters. A materialized view for a single email is impractical. Warehouse size doesn't improve full table scans.

text: GET_DDL('TABLE', 'my_table') returns the CREATE TABLE DDL including all column definitions and properties. Does this function require a running warehouse?

options.A: Yes — all SQL functions require warehouse compute
options.B: No — GET_DDL retrieves metadata from the Cloud Services layer without warehouse compute
options.C: Yes — but only for tables with more than 100 columns
options.D: No — but it charges 1 Cloud Services credit per call

explanation: GET_DDL is a metadata operation handled entirely by Cloud Services. Like SHOW, DESCRIBE, EXPLAIN, and metadata-based COUNT(*), it requires no warehouse. Cloud Services handles metadata queries at no additional per-call credit cost (included in the CS layer budget).

text: A query uses: SELECT dept, ARRAY_AGG(DISTINCT name) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY name) FROM emp GROUP BY dept. What is the return type of ARRAY_AGG?

options.A: VARCHAR — a comma-separated string
options.B: ARRAY (stored as VARIANT) — an ordered JSON array of distinct values
options.C: OBJECT — a key-value mapping
options.D: TABLE — a nested rowset

explanation: ARRAY_AGG returns an ARRAY type (stored as VARIANT containing a JSON array). WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY) orders the elements. DISTINCT deduplicates. LISTAGG returns VARCHAR. OBJECT_AGG returns OBJECT. Snowflake does not have nested TABLE types.

text: A table was loaded chronologically (January data first, December data last) with NO explicit clustering key. A query filtering WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2025-06-01' AND '2025-06-30' prunes 11 out of 12 months of data. Why is pruning effective without a clustering key?

options.A: Snowflake automatically creates hidden clustering keys on date columns
options.B: Natural data ordering from chronological loading creates aligned micro-partition ranges, enabling implicit pruning
options.C: The query optimizer dynamically re-clusters data at query time
options.D: BETWEEN filters always achieve full pruning regardless of data layout

explanation: Data loaded in chronological order creates micro-partitions with naturally non-overlapping date ranges. Snowflake's metadata (min/max per partition) can prune partitions outside the query's date range. No explicit clustering key is needed — the natural load order provides 'implicit clustering'. Snowflake does not auto-create hidden keys.

text: EXPLAIN is run on a COUNT(*) query against a large table. The plan shows only a 'Result' node with no TableScan. What does this mean?

options.A: The query failed to compile
options.B: The query is answered entirely from metadata (row count statistics) without scanning any data
options.C: EXPLAIN is not supported for aggregate queries
options.D: The query was blocked by a security policy

explanation: A plan with only 'Result' and no TableScan means the query can be answered from metadata alone. COUNT(*) on a table uses the metadata cache's stored row count, requiring no data scan and no warehouse. This is the most efficient execution possible.

text: A Query Profile shows a Hash Join operator with the note: 'Build side: DIMENSION_TABLE (broadcast)'. What does 'broadcast' mean in this context?

options.A: The dimension table results are sent to all nodes as a network broadcast message
options.B: The smaller dimension table is fully replicated to every compute node to avoid redistributing the larger fact table
options.C: The join result is broadcast to all downstream operators
options.D: The dimension table is stored in a broadcast-replicated cache layer

explanation: In a broadcast join, the smaller table (build side) is fully replicated to all nodes processing the larger table (probe side). This avoids the expensive network shuffle of the large table. The optimizer automatically chooses broadcast when the size asymmetry makes it efficient.

text: A developer writes: SELECT TRY_CAST('abc' AS INTEGER). What is returned?

options.B: An error: invalid numeric value
options.C: NULL
options.D: The string 'abc'

explanation: TRY_CAST returns NULL when the conversion fails instead of raising an error. Regular CAST('abc' AS INTEGER) would produce an error. TRY_CAST is the safe alternative for type conversions where values might be invalid. It always returns NULL on failure.

text: A MERGE INTO statement fails with 'Non-deterministic MERGE detected' error. What is the cause and what is the default parameter controlling this behavior?

options.A: Target table has no PRIMARY KEY; parameter: REQUIRE_PRIMARY_KEY = TRUE
options.B: Multiple source rows match the same target row; parameter: ERROR_ON_NONDETERMINISTIC_MERGE defaults to TRUE
options.C: The MERGE has conflicting WHEN MATCHED and WHEN NOT MATCHED clauses; no parameter controls this
options.D: The source and target are the same table; parameter: ALLOW_SELF_MERGE = FALSE

explanation: When multiple source rows match a single target row, the update result is non-deterministic. ERROR_ON_NONDETERMINISTIC_MERGE defaults to TRUE, raising an error. Fix: deduplicate the source to ensure 1:1 matching. PRIMARY KEY is informational-only in Snowflake. Self-merges are allowed.

text: A provider shares a secure view that joins tables from DB1 and DB2. The view is in DB1. The provider adds the view to the share but the consumer gets an error accessing it. What privilege is missing?

options.A: SELECT on the DB2 tables granted to the share
options.B: REFERENCE_USAGE on DB2 granted to the share
options.C: USAGE on DB2 granted to the share
options.D: Cross-database views cannot be shared

explanation: When a shared secure view references objects in a different database, REFERENCE_USAGE must be granted on that other database to the share. This is a special privilege for cross-database sharing. Regular USAGE is insufficient. The grant must be: GRANT REFERENCE_USAGE ON DATABASE DB2 TO SHARE my_share.

text: A provider drops a column from a table that is actively shared. A consumer has a report querying that column. What does the consumer experience?

options.A: The consumer's query returns NULL for the dropped column
options.B: The consumer's query fails because the column no longer exists in the live shared object
options.C: The consumer retains the column until they refresh their database
options.D: The provider cannot drop the column while the table is in an active share

explanation: Shares are live — consumers always see the current state. Dropped columns immediately disappear. Consumer queries referencing the dropped column fail with a 'column not found' error. There is no snapshot, caching, or prevention of schema changes on shared objects.

text: A consumer receives shared data and runs: SELECT s.*, l.* FROM shared_db.schema.shared_table s JOIN local_db.schema.local_table l ON s.id = l.id. Is this query valid?

options.A: No — shared and local data cannot be joined in the same query
options.B: Yes — consumers can freely join shared tables with their own local tables
options.C: No — joins require both tables to be in the same database
options.D: Yes — but only if both tables are in the same schema

explanation: Consumers can freely join shared tables with their own local tables in any query. Cross-database JOINs between shared and local databases work normally. There are no restrictions on mixing shared and local data in SELECT, JOIN, UNION, or subqueries.

text: Account A creates a failover group replicating database DB1 to Account B. A disaster disables Account A. An administrator in Account B promotes the failover group. What is the state of DB1 in Account B after promotion?

options.A: DB1 remains read-only — failover groups only provide read access
options.B: DB1 becomes read-write in Account B — the failover group is promoted to primary
options.C: DB1 is deleted and must be restored from a backup
options.D: Account B must wait for Account A to recover before accessing DB1

explanation: When a failover group is promoted, the target account becomes the primary. DB1 becomes fully read-write in Account B. This is the key difference between replication groups (always read-only) and failover groups (can be promoted to read-write for disaster recovery).

text: A Reader Account admin runs CREATE SHARE to share data with another partner. What happens?

options.A: The share is created successfully
options.B: The command fails — Reader Accounts cannot create shares; they can only consume shared data
options.C: The share is created but requires approval from the parent provider account
options.D: The command fails because Reader Accounts cannot create any objects at all

explanation: Reader Accounts are consumption-only for sharing purposes. They CAN create objects like warehouses, tables, views, and users for their own use, but they CANNOT create shares (act as providers). Only full Snowflake accounts can create and manage outbound shares.
